# 

# 1\. Background and Problem Description {#1.-background-and-problem-description}

## 1.1. Terminology {#1.1.-terminology}

One usage note \-  
It is common for people to use the terms “Front End” and “Back End” to refer to roles.  These terms are confusing and misleading.  This doc will not use them and would like to discourage their use.  In this context the proper terms are Edge Serial and Long Tail.  In other contexts other more correct terms might be used.

### 1.1.1. Terminology introduced in this design {#1.1.1.-terminology-introduced-in-this-design}

**Large Region:** For this discussion, a Large Region is a region capable of serving a large amount of end user demand, with the initial proposals being in the 10Tbps range. Large Regions for this design could be above or below the old 64 server limit, the may be in a single rack, span racks or be in a Fabric deployment.  
**Region Distributed Cache (RDC):** The solution proposed in this document. It involves a division of the cache into a cache for objects popular with end user requests and one for the long tail of all content.  
**Edge Serial Cache:** The set of objects that are popular with end user requests.  
**Long Tail Cache:** The set of objects that are distributed across the region to improve the overall efficiency of the cache  
**Edge Serial**: The serial used by traditional Mapping/DNSP to assign traffic to the region.  The same as the current Edge Serials published in DNS to end users.   
**GOPS Serial / Long Tail Serial:**  A non-overlapping serial range used by GOPS to assign cacheable traffic to machines in a region.  GOPS Serials will eventually be on a new subservice.  
**GOPS / Ghost Object Placement System:** A system for distributing content across a set of servers via a deterministic hash based IP selection system  
**Content Shard / Shard Index:** An index selected based on a hash similar to the Ghost cache key hash that assigns a particular object to bucket GOPS.    
**GRCP / Ghost Remote Cache Protocol:** An as yet unimplemented protocol for efficient fetching from Ghost caches without applying ARL metadata.  
**Edge Serial Server**: The machine serving end user traffic.   
**Long Tail Server**: The machine serving distributed cache traffic to Edge Serial servers   
**Subservice**: (from DNSP) A service whose load is reported and balanced separately from the main service on a network, but whose overall load is managed by RA/SRMM as part of the main service.  In Freeflow the W service is the main service, the GOPS service will be a subservice of W.

### 1.1.2. Terminology for existing implementations {#1.1.2.-terminology-for-existing-implementations}

**Content Sharding:**  Akamai asks customers to distribute their content across a set of host names in order to reduce both per serial and per-maprule demand.  The sharded content may be distributed to multiple serials, multiple maprules or both depending on the amount of demand the customer has for their content.  
**HSAF / Hash Serial And Forward:** An existing Ghost implementation that distributes content by tunneling to a machine in the region on a new serial computed by hashing the request URI.  
**P-ICP / Probabilistic ICP:** An existing Ghost feature that allows Ghost to decide based on a probability whether a particular object will be cached or discarded after it is fetched and delivered. Often combined with HSAF to limit object copies for  unpopular objects.  
**HSAR / Hash Serial And Redirect:** An existing ARL based solution that hashes URLs and generates redirects to new Akamai locations in order to aggregate demand for particular objects on servers likely to have that object in cache.  
**Ping Likely Peers:** A Ghost ICP feature where ICP queries (pings) are sent only to machines currently in a specific serial spread. It can be augmented to include the next several IPs in the serial spread and to cover some cases of serial overlap. The feature is frequently disabled in product templates and customer ARL files.  
**Bloom Filter:** An existing Ghost feature that looks up a cache key in a bloom filter to decide whether that object has enough hits to be written to disk. When Bloom Filter is enabled objects are admitted on the second hit.  
**HSAF Serial:** A serial in the existing DNSP managed range, typically a subset of the standard 1-2047 range. This is used by HSAF to distribute content in a region.    
**Midgress**: As used in this document midgress is traffic between Akamai regions.  This is typically edge region to cache parent region traffic.  
**Full Stack Request**: A full stack request is one where full customer ARL configuration is applied and all feature checks are made while serving content. This is costly in terms of CPU usage and prevents many optimizations.  In most cases the feature set applied to an Edge request is very different from the set applied to a Ghost to Ghost request.

### 1.1.3. Terminology from Split Ghost {#1.1.3.-terminology-from-split-ghost}

**Monolithic Ghost:** The traditional Ghost process model where all features reside in a single process.  The Ghost we are running as of July 2021\.  
**Split Ghost:** A modernization of Ghost where we run at least three processes instead of one single process, with those processes having specific functionality.  A further evolution of the implementation will have Dedicated Server Roles where servers run role specific software to allow further COGS optimizations.  
**TLS Endpoint (or Endpoint):** In the three process model of Split Ghost this is the process that handles TLS negotiations with remote machines, either as server or client.  
**Protocol Processor:** In the three process Split Ghost model, the process that implements all of the customer business logic and protocol-based features.  
**Ghost Object Cache:** In the three process Split Ghost model, the process that implements all of the cache management for the server. This includes all cache disk IO, footprint management, purge and cache eviction.  
**FPS / Feature Processing Server:** In the Split Ghost dedicated server roles model this is the server role that applies customer processing features to incoming requests. Sometimes called a Front End in the context of Split Ghost.  
**CES / Cache Extension Server:** In Split Ghost dedicated server roles model this is the server role that caches content for FPS regions. Sometimes called a Back End in the context of Split Ghost.

## 1.2. The Problems {#1.2.-the-problems}

Large Regions are for the purposes of this design regions capable of serving large unsharded customer demand.  This design does not require a specific server count, hardware deployment or server type.  For deployment purposes, Large Regions could be a single rack of high capacity servers, a region that spans racks, or a region in a Fabric deployment.  The design proposed here should scale well to any size region, from a small single rack AANP to a Fabric deployment with many servers. It will lack some of the efficiency gains of a complete Split Ghost Dedicated Server Roles implementation but is designed to perform well.  For the Tech Preview we may be using a region that is in a Fabric deployment, using the new Bison server model. 

We need to start making Large Regions work well in 2021 with the existing Monolithic Ghost software stack. The changes we make to support Large Regions need to scale well for all region sizes to replace existing cross-machine cache sharing schemes. These changes need to be forward looking to leverage as many future Split Ghost features as possible, with as little specialized work as possible.  Eventually these solutions will be used in places where deployment of large Split Ghost FPS and CES server clusters is not possible.  This will include AANP deployments, and smaller scale deployments where there may be significant traffic but dedicated role servers cannot be deployed. The system will support regions from small to arbitrarily large server counts.

In addition to the long term design goals, we have a Tech Preview plan for 2021 that intends to support four large demand customers delivering unsharded content from a single region.  These customers are Amazon, Hulu, Disney and Bytedance.  This Tech Preview will take some shortcuts from the long term solution we are proposing. The shortcuts will be made to get a system in place for testing by the end of 2021\.  That testing will be used to generate data that can refine designs and improve large region scale.  The Tech Preview will have efficiency losses because of the shortcuts.  Each of these losses will be quantifiable as part of the Tech Preview’s reporting.  There will be a clear roadmap to eliminate these inefficiencies, while at the same time gaining important information about where to concentrate our efforts.

We can’t use the existing Ghost cache sharing features for Large Regions. The current in-region cache sharing mechanisms of ICP+HTTP/S, with HSAF or HSAF \+ P-ICP added on for some customers scales poorly as region sizes grow.  Using these features adds extra delivery latency and can cause in-region traffic amplifications. We need to develop a new implementation that minimizes the amount of cache duplication and improves offload while improving performance. Any improvement in region-wide offload will be reflected in better overall end user performance for cached objects. 

If you are aware of the issues with the existing large region solutions available in Ghost feel free to skip to [Section 2](#2.-solution-proposal).

For the Arch Group review, please skim these sections, but we want to spend as little time as possible in the review discussing the problems with the current Ghost solutions.  The rest of this section exists to summarize the existing issues, not to create a lengthy debate about those issues.

### 1.2.1. Customer content sharding  {#1.2.1.-customer-content-sharding}

We currently require customers with large demand or large object footprints to publish content on many host headers.  Each of these host headers resolves to a different Akamai serial number and potentially a different map rule as well. We are the only CDN that insists on this practice which makes it a competitive disadvantage for us.  Customer dislike for host name sharding is significant and can cause them to remove content from Akamai.

We ask customers to shard to control how much demand is assigned to a single DNS resolution result. We need to do this because our individual servers have limited capacity compared to customer demand so assigning all content to a single host name can easily overload the machines returned in the DNS resolution.  There is a limit to the number of IPs we can return in DNS so for any region the capacity for unsharded content is (IPs returned \* machine capacity).  DNSP will vary the returned IPs if demand exceeds this capacity but that causes load shifts in the region every time DNS TTLs expire.  Sharding by serial number keeps most demand within the limits of the IPs returned by a stable DNS resolution.

Sharding by maprule happens when the demand of a host name is larger than the capacity of entire regions regardless of how serials are sharded. The host name is split up into host names whose demand is small enough for regions in the maps it uses.   
Large regions with much higher capacities are needed to eliminate these sharding requirements, but they don’t scale well for many reasons.

### 1.2.2. Common issues with in-region caching {#1.2.2.-common-issues-with-in-region-caching}

In region caching is not well distributed.  With the current implementation, cache spread is tied to DNSP’s load spreading algorithm.  DNSP can only manage spreading flit load in order to keep machines from being overloaded on one of the three resources, CPU, disk or network flits. The default minimum spread is 2 which attempts to assign whatever cache footprint needed to serve that load into two servers.  When DNSP needs to spread load because of higher CPU or network demand it forces Ghosts in the region to make more copies of objects.  The more that a serial spread, the more likely it is that Akamai configurations will try to take measures to prevent the creation of these duplicates. DNSP assigns load to servers in regions based on the best fit the aggregate flit usage of the serial.  

A typical observed DNSP behavior in an edge map region is to assign small object demand with high CPU costs into one set of servers, to assign high network or disk demand into another set and have the rest of the region mix these two demands to some degree.  This causes footprints with high object counts and high flit cost traffic to accumulate on one set of servers and footprints with a low count of large objects to accumulate on another set, with some set that mixes the two to some degree.  Each type of object is spread over a smaller set of servers than might best serve the caching demands.  All servers have higher cache churn than is ideal and either object count or disk space exhaustion. This is an intentional and well understood behavior of DNSP, since it assigns load by flit demand without a direct understanding of cache usage.  
DNSP ties cache locality to flit load locality and tries to minimize cache spread by minimizing load spread. As a result regions do not use cache efficiently for any load type. To offset this we have created several features that are intended to spread cache to varying degrees but they all have shortcomings. 

Additional complications come from the fact that we mix loads in relatively unpredictable ways in regions.  Mapping’s Region Assignment via RA, SRMM or other mapping assignment systems and low level load balancing via DNSP is designed to make different assignments of regions for each maprule, and different serial assignments within each region. The assignments often change, potentially at every map assignment and serial assignment cycle based on demand.  Thus each region serves a different maprule mix and each server in the region serves different portions of that load mix even when two regions might have the same maprules assigned to them.  This unpredictable mixing makes simple solutions for object and footprint management difficult to get right.  Every Ghost has different cache pressures and simple policies have a hard time accounting for the broad range of differences over time. Future features need to be adaptable to a huge variety of unpredictable network conditions without manual tuning or fixed policies.

Finally, we need to make the proposed solutions work when mixed with existing network deployments like Metrocache, and with existing customer configs that attempt to solve sharding and distribution problems in ARL metadata. We need to plan how any new implementations interact with the existing deployments and configurations without defeating existing customer delivery optimizations or our internal network management. The new features need to be understandable and manageable by the teams responsible for network configurations, with a long term goal of fully automated configuration with minimal human management that yields uniformly good performance.

### 1.2.3. Detailed description of the issues with current implementations {#1.2.3.-detailed-description-of-the-issues-with-current-implementations}

The current features of ICP, HSAF, HSAR and P-ICP are designed to distribute footprint and demand in regions and minimize duplication of unpopular objects at the long tail of the popularity distribution.  They work reasonably well but do not scale well as region sizes increase or they require too much manual configuration and customer interaction.  We have expended considerable engineering and services to implement and improve these features to work but we still see issues where they don’t work well when combined with other features like Partial Object Caching (POC) and various forms of prefetching.  The section below will describe the shortcomings of each of the existing implementations.  

In general the scaling problems fall into the following categories:

* Poor CPU use scaling as server counts increase, in some cases O(n) scaling which makes increases in server count lose efficiency.  
* Increases in back end (10.x.x.x) network traffic as local cache miss rates increase. This can also scale with server count, meaning small increases in servers over a threshold causes much larger amounts of back end network traffic.  
* Decreases in system stability as shifts in serial mapping to servers can cause large movements of content footprints and increased miss rates.  
* Increases in disk usage imbalances as either CPU or disk intensive traffic is aggregated on smaller sets of servers/  
* Decreased delivery performance as more CPU is used and more traffic traverses the back end.

#### 1.2.3.1. ICP Issues {#1.2.3.1.-icp-issues}

ICP is a discovery protocol that asks other servers in the region whether they have a particular cache key in cache. The initiator sends a simply formatted UDP packet (a ping) with the cache key and other information.  If  the receiver replies with an ICP HIT response an HTTP request can be issued to fetch the content. There are many issues with this system:

* Sending, receiving and processing all of the ICP requests is a significant overhead for most machines. Most ICP pings result in a cache miss so this CPU does not have a good return on investment.  This can be 25% of the CPU used in a busy Ghost serving a small object workload.  
* The number of pings needed scales poorly as the serial spreads in a region and as the number of servers in the region increases. This can be constrained by serial spread but often is not.  The normal method of constraint adds five extra IPs to the ping list. If the serial has not spread this is 5x the beneficial workload on senders and receivers.  
* Caching is subject to Edge serial expansion and contraction, where content is cached on more servers during busy periods and fewer servers when traffic is low. Some customers have very large variations in traffic volumes and serial spreads over a day.  If we limit the number of servers pinged to the current serial spread \+ some fixed number we will miss content at low traffic times and over-pinging at high traffic times.  
* Limiting ICP to pinging only the current serial spread plus a few extra is frequently disabled in Media configurations in order to find all possible content in a region.  This can reduce missed content and reduce midgress.  Pinging all peers floods servers with extra pings greatly increasing CPU consumption.   
* ICP MISS responses were turned off because the cost of sending and receiving them was quite large.  Most ICP responses were ICP MISSes and processing the queries caused head of line blocking for the significantly more valuable ICP HIT responses.  For “ping all peers” ICP in a 40 machine region, on a serial that spread to 2 IPs there were likely 37 ICP MISS responses for every potential ICP HIT.    
* Without an ICP\_MISS Ghost must wait some time period for a response to an ICP\_QUERY before giving up on the possibility of an ICP hit and going forward out of the region. This delay has recently been optimized but still can be several milliseconds.    
* Since ICP is a discovery protocol there is always a chance that the content is unavailable and all of the pinging and it’s associated CPU use and delay is a complete waste. In a long tail workload the likelihood of a miss is much higher so timeout delays are seen more often.  
* An ICP HIT could be a hit on a stale object.  Accessing this object would drive an out of region fetch and make a fresh copy on two servers. New implementations exist which ignore stale objects in the region in favor of an out of region fetch to have only one fresh copy. When P-ICP is enabled this optimization needs to be disabled. The stale objects are likely to be the only copy in the region and ignoring them would create unwanted duplicates.  
* Disk flits and CPU flits used to deliver objects in the region reduce the flits available to deliver end user traffic.  This can be as high as 25% of all disk flits. Recent experiments have shown that there is no performance degradation in MCH if ICP is disabled and an MCH edge machine goes to an MCH parent with comparable disk performance.  This suggests that we could simply disable ICP for MCH edge traffic and gain back all of the flits it uses for edge delivery.

ICP does have the advantage of being a lightweight request and response with minimal parsing overhead and little work needed to look up and reply compared to an HTTP request. The CPU cost is low for individual queries and responses.  If the pinging can be properly scoped to minimize unnecessary queries it is a reasonable feature.  ICP can be useful to get a low latency “in cache \+ stale/fresh” response answer in some of the flows we will propose. In certain cases we might want to always get an ICP\_MISS or ICP\_HIT answer to reduce timeouts, but as ICP is currently used it is too costly to enable ICP miss responses because of the low hit rates.

#### 1.2.3.2. HSAF Issues {#1.2.3.2.-hsaf-issues}

HSAF is a method of spreading content within a region by selecting a new serial number based on a hash of the object URL. The content is only cached on machines in the new serial.  An edge machine will tunnel on the back end to a machine in the new HSAF forward serial with a full stack HTTP request. That request may then trigger an ICP ping and fetch that involves yet another server in the region.  It is often combined with P-ICP where the ICP responses are not cached for unpopular objects to limit duplication. 

* Each server does a full stack Ghost request with all ARL metadata applied which is very CPU-expensive.  At least one request doesn’t cache the response which is very costly.  
* Using P-ICP makes the chance of hitting three machines high. This means that there are three full stack requests, some number of ICP pings and two requests that discard the response when they are complete.  
* Small object workloads with high Edge request rates generate a high multiplier effect on flit usage.  
* As with ICP, disk and CPU flits used to deliver objects in the region reduce the flits available to deliver end user traffic. For HSAF this can be a significant amount of CPU.  
* Machine suspensions and Ghost rolls can cause large shifts in footprint.  The number of hash buckets is limited to a small number in most HSAF configurations so any movement of serials to new machines will move a large percentage of the traffic.  When serials move the miss rate spikes, forward requests increase and cache duplication is increased.  
* Enough traffic movement can overload regions before DNSP can respond and can trigger cascading Ghost rolls.

#### 1.2.3.3. Probabilistic ICP issues {#1.2.3.3.-probabilistic-icp-issues}

Probabilistic ICP is a system that applies a probability to in-region fetches to determine whether a response should be cached.  Probabilities are typically 90% bypass / 10% keep.  For long tail content with limited popularity this feature keeps a single copy of most objects in a region unless they have a reasonable number of hits on at least two machines.  P-ICP is frequently combined with other features like various types of prefetching, POC or HSAF to distribute long tail content in a region without creating extra duplicates of requests with low popularity.   These are the issues with P-ICP:

* Many of the HSAF and ICP issues also apply here \- doubling the number of full-stack requests for cache hits, expensive full-stack metadata and high ICP overhead for bits not cached, serial shifts can cause content discovery loss.  
* It can be difficult to combine correctly with other features.  As late as 2020 there were bugs that meant it did not work properly with POC  
* Improper use of the “no-store” option of P-ICP can cause cache evictions of content we want to keep.  
* The cache/bypass/no-store probabilities are fixed in ARL configurations.  Setting them is mostly by heuristic and is not at all adaptive or easily tuned for particular workloads or network hops. For example, we might want edges to have a different popularity threshold than parents, and TD1/CW might be different from TD0/MCH.  
* P-ICP can often impose a two hop Edge minimum which increases TTFB.  
* Many streaming customers use range requests to fetch content .  Multiple range requests by a single client can artificially inflate popularity to the point where all objects look popular, when each byte is only requested once per client.

Despite these shortcomings P-ICP is quite efficient at achieving the design goals when the appropriate traffic profile is presented. It is very effective at reducing copies of unpopular objects while maximizing copies of objects that are reasonably popular.  The key drawbacks are issues with feature interaction bugs, the lack of easy tunability, susceptibility to fail false positives with some traffic, and its reliance on the inefficient ICP discovery protocol.

#### 1.2.3.4. HSAR issues {#1.2.3.4.-hsar-issues}

Hash Serial And Redirect (HSAR) is a system to use Ghost metadata to distribute content. When Ghost receives an end-user ARL configurations execute a hash on the URL.  The hash result is used to generate a redirect response that uses a new host header to send the client to a new location.  Since each URL has the same hash result requests for objects get redirected to servers and content is effectively sharded and partitioned. This solution can work well but also has issues that make it unpopular.

* Customers have to accept the extra latency created by the redirect and the clients have to be able to correctly chase redirects to the new location.  This can be a significant performance penalty.  
* The extra requests generate extra CPU costs for Ghost. The requests that get redirected have a processing cost. All stream and content security features should be applied before generating redirects and again when serving redirected content.  
* Customers have to provision the additional host headers and any certificates or other resources needed for the redirect to work successfully.  
* Akamai has to provision and manage maps for the redirect targets.  
* The ARL metadata has to be carefully designed to make the redirects work correctly.  The appropriate static portions of the URI need to be included, where portions that vary per request or per end user must be excluded to prevent random distribution.    
* Each customer must be configured individually.  There are common patterns but each customer’s exact usage needs to be tested to make sure the solution works correctly.   
* Customer URI usage can change over time and break the distribution hash.  
* Popular content will spread on the redirect target maprules and serials, potentially overloading machines and diminishing the content locality benefit.  
* There is no guarantee that the redirected traffic will go the same location as the initial requests which can affect delivery performance.

HSAR is usable but is not popular either with customers or Akamai because it has a high implementation and maintenance cost.

# 2\. Solution Proposal {#2.-solution-proposal}

## 2.1. Detailed goals {#2.1.-detailed-goals}

The solution needs to balance the needs of edge hit rates and the desire to avoid sending too much traffic over the back end network.  The traffic that goes over the back end needs to consume as little CPU as possible. Latency additions in the new system should be minimized to a limited set of circumstances.

This solution is specifically targeted at large regions capable of handling unsharded customers. Most if not all of the details will apply equally well to smaller deployments, but this design specifically does not discuss AANP style small deployments.   
A more detailed list of specific goals is:

1. **Improve bit load and cache locality correlation.** Serve bytes from the first server that sees the request where possible.  If serving from the first server is not possible, make only one subsequent hop in the region.  That hop should be as fast as possible at as low a resource cost as possible. This will provide the best end user performance in the largest number of cases.  
2. **Locate objects, don’t discover them.** Eliminate ICP’s discovery protocol and the attendant CPU consumption, scale issues and latencies in favor of a location system that does not require a content discovery step. This will reduce CPU costs and improve latency.  
3. **Spread objects independent of load demands.** Effectively spread cache in a region independent of the Edge serial spread to enable better  cache use and offload.  This will allow us to cache more objects for each customer and evict more fairly across the full customer set.  
4. **Minimize in region object duplication.** Properly balance cache duplication for popular objects and non-duplication for unpopular objects.  This will allow better overall cache usage across all re-use distributions. An important insight is that making copies of popular objects improves end user performance by reducing server hops.  For these objects having extra copies is a tradeoff we may want to make.  
5. **Improve region-wide offload.** Provide better offload for the Edge serials and Edge regions, reduce midgress and origin traffic. This will improve end user performance by avoiding higher latency midgress, reduce midgress costs and increase origin offload.  
6. **Minimize bytes served in-region where possible.** Avoid creating an HSAF like solution that requires all misses to use the back end to get content.  Avoid back end traffic amplification from ineffective prefetching and client range fetches.  
7. **Eliminate unnecessary full-stack requests where possible.** Eliminate the use of full-stack requests for as many in-region fetches as possible in order to save CPU and reduce response times.  
8. **Provide measurement to improve future implementations.** Gather data and experience for full separation of caches on to dedicated servers  
9. **Don’t limit usable scope to large regions.** These solutions must work well in small deployments like a single region AANP.  This design will not discuss smaller deployments to try to limit the scope of the discussion.  All proposed features will work in smaller deployments.  
10. **Minimize work that is not usable in later enhancements.** This design tries to avoid implementations that will be discarded at the end of the project.   
11. **Provide Tech Preview solution in 2021\.** The intent of this design is to describe the full design work, then to state how the Tech Preview will proceed without having implemented all of the features.

With these goals in mind we have designed a solution.

## 2.2. Topics not in scope for this document {#2.2.-topics-not-in-scope-for-this-document}

This document addresses the Ghost implementation specifically. Several items are not discussed and should be considered out of scope for the design review.  These items are relevant for the Large Regions project and the final Region Distributed Cache implementation but will not be discussed in this document.

1. Any hardware deployment or networking considerations. This includes whether the deployment spans traditional racks or is in a Fabric deployment and any discussions of in-region bandwidth.  The design assumes that server to server bandwidth is available and not constrained by Top Of Rack switches in the case of servers spanning multiple racks.   
2. Any details or discussion of sub-service load reporting or load balancing, the size and availability of new DNSP messages, the size of the new GOPS serial range and how IPs are assigned to the new GOPS serials. These are pertinent details to the full implementation and are necessary for the system to be successful. This design assumes that the new load reporting and load balancing mechanisms exist and work well, and that a new message of unspecified size exists and is usable.  
3. Any discussion of what hardware will be used in the Tech Preview or in long term deployments, memory and disk sizes and CPU counts.  This solution is intended to be agnostic to these considerations outside of configurations to run properly on whatever hardware is available.  
4. Any specific claims of performance, offload, TTFB or other metrics.  We will make measurements of all of these metrics as part of the development and rollout of the RDC system. We will not make specific claims of expected improvements in any metric at this point in the design.  Without data it is hard to make any sensible claims to improvements.  We expect the system to perform well but there are areas where resource consumption will be less than ideal during the Tech Preview.  Designs and enhancement plans exist to address these expected areas of higher than ideal resource use. Prioritization of resource and performance issue mitigations will be driven by measurements of the system behaviors.  
5. ESSL support. In particular discussions about Edge Serial to Long Tail connection usage related to customer cert usage and request smuggling are not addressed.  These are known issues that will have to be resolved before we move forward with any ESSL RDC implementations.

## 2.3. Solution Overview {#2.3.-solution-overview}

The solution proposed is to split each server’s disk cache into two logical allocations that evict independently. A portion of the cache on each machine will be reserved to cache objects requested by requests on Edge Serials.  This will be called the Edge Serial Cache.  The remainder will be used to cache Long Tail content.  This will be called the Long Tail Cache.  The Edge Serial Cache will be a cache for popular objects that mapping may need to spread broadly across the region because of the demand for that footprint.  The Long Tail Cache will aggregate caching for objects to specific servers so that the full footprint is spread across the region to use all cache most efficiently.  The size of the Edge Serial cache will be limited by both disk blocks and by object count so that it does not overwhelm the Long Tail on either metric.

The Edge Serial Cache will have a system managing disk IO so that it does not generate excessive disk IO or cache a large number of one hit objects. It will also have implementations to prevent back end traffic amplifications caused by prefetching and other Ghost features.

A new object placement system called GOPS will be used to determine which server(s) in the region is the correct location for objects in the cache. On an edge cache miss machines will fetch from the machines indicated by GOPS.  All requests in the region will aggregate to the GOPS target thus limiting the creation of extra copies, and distributing caching across all servers in the region.

In the long term solution Ghost will use an efficient object fetch and push protocol called GRCP to transfer objects between machines in the region.  This will save the CPU cost of a full stack Ghost request on Long Tail servers.  In the short term GRCP will not be available so normal full stack requests will be used to populate the Long Tail cache.  The excess flit usage of this system will be measured as part of the Tech Preview. Without GRCP we still have many benefits of cache distribution and reduced replication. GRCP is the protocol we expect to use 

This system will address the goals described in section 2.1.  Content will be located, not discovered, popular content will be cached where end user demand appears.  The long tail will be distributed across all servers in the region to better use disk and improve region offload. We will have an efficient transfer mechanism that will minimize the CPU flit cost of the in-region transfers. All of the major work will transfer directly to the larger Split Ghost project, but will also work well in any size of traditional Ghost region.

### 2.3.1. Additional Documents {#2.3.1.-additional-documents}

These documents can be considered additional background information for the design. Although they are not required reading they will explain details that are not delved into here.  They are all referenced in the text at appropriate points where their detail might be useful.  Some of these are works in progress, with details still to be settled before the final implementation.

1. [How Shards Spread](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1o1YQvyJn3HnhBRzN6S2N1VXavlO82zs2gOXPTrgPzUY/edit#)  A design from Mangesh Kasbekar that describes the GOPS shard spreading design.  
2. [Region Distributed Cache Metadata and Metrocache Interactions](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1IcOQdEPfoVOfAlnd5zMWHREAMZtZQfhxLzgKk4qArCw/edit#)A document that describes how RDC metadata is structured and how it is related to MCH metadata.  
3. [Region Distributed Cache Amplification Avoidance](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ImFDLXPt8_1kUo5taipP5phEOKP_4wGPIM0Ros4uuGA/edit#) A document that has details about the problems with amplification we see today, and that are likely to persist with the RDC design. The design proposes solutions for the problems that allow for more efficient use of region resources.  
4. [GOPS Shard Index Construction And Ghost's Cache Key](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1D3Sjr_EMfDnDoDzP9t9QN6sZ4ohGRJNedikLwmoj3N8/edit)A design document that shows how the GOPS Shard index will be constructed.  This takes into consideration how the current cache key would distribute objects, and how a GOPS hash should work.  
5. [Region Distributed Cache Entrance Criteria](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1hUWlfTv6flyEqN41htxVkvTZq1wgYKUDYXVwWEx5nRg/edit) A design for cache entrance criteria for the Edge Serial cache.   
6. [Ghost Remote Cache Protocol](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1LNDPLVpo6vP5C3GXx953ZWeBfzv-SxBHb0_DXv4XT4k/edit)A design for the Ghost Remote Cache Protocol by [Shweta Nandakumar](mailto:snandaku@akamai.com). This document describes in detail how the proposed new protocol will work.

These detailed documents will be used as the basis for actual Ghost implementation work for Region Distributed Cache.  More detailed docs are likely to be produced to cover any work not described in these documents.

## 2.4. Solution Details {#2.4.-solution-details}

![][image1]  
 Edge Serial Cache Diagram

## 

For the discussion below any request arriving in a region from outside, whether it is from an end user or another Ghost via a cache parent map of some sort is considered an Edge Serial request.  Long Tail requests are requests that stay in the region for objects cached on Long Tail servers. Long Tail requests will never come from outside of the region.  Edge Serial requests will always come from outside of the region except for prefetching requests and other similar internal requests driven by Ghost features.  Barring any error handling, any out of region requests should hit at the most two servers, one Edge Serial and one Long Tail.  All servers in the region will act in both roles and will be capable of handling requests from outside of the region.  There are no special server roles planned in this implementation.

### 2.4.1. Edge Serial and Long Tail Caches {#2.4.1.-edge-serial-and-long-tail-caches}

Footprint identifiers are groupings of objects in the Ghost cache.  When an object is served a Footprint Identifier is assigned based on a set of rules that classify traffic by several possible attributes.  These can include maprule, hostname and ARLID among others.  We will add a new attribute for the classification, whether the request is an Edge Serial or Long Tail request. On any machine the objects in cache will be part of either an Edge Serial footprint, or they will be part of a Long Tail footprint.  The Edge Serial and Long Tail caches are not implemented as independent caches on disk or independent Store Tables in Ghost.  There will only be one object cache implementation and store table on the machines.  Any object will exist only once on any machine that should cache that object. The object will be marked as either part of the Edge Serial cache or the Long Tail cache depending on how it has been accessed.  In the Ghost Object Cache implementation there will be only a small change to manage the attributes of Edge Serial or Long Tail via the existing [Footprint ID system](https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/pages/viewpage.action?spaceKey=~snandaku&title=Ghost+Footprint+Management). The Ghost Object Cache code will implement the Edge Serial Cache by selecting a single special Footprint Identifier that marks objects as Edge Serial objects based on attributes of the request. 

### 2.4.2. GOPS and Content Shards {#2.4.2.-gops-and-content-shards}

When an Edge Serial request arrives Ghost will calculate a Content Shard Index. If there is a cache miss or refresh is needed the Shard Index will be used to decide the Long Tail location(s) for the object using GOPS, the Ghost Object Placement System.  If the object’s Long Tail location is the current server the object will be fetched or refreshed from out of the region and cached marked as a Long Tail object.  If the Long Tail Location is not the current server then the Long Tail server will be contacted for the object and that server will execute the out of region fetch if necessary.  Before Edge Serial objects are written to the disk an Edge Serial Cache Entrance Criteria will be applied.  Only objects that pass this criteria will be persisted to disk.  This will prevent overrunning the Edge Serial cache with one hit wonders and driving excess disk IO.  Long Tail object replication in the region will be constrained in this design by having a very granular set of Content Shards.  A single very busy shard will have a smaller traffic impact and spread copies of fewer objects to as many machines as needed to satisfy the flit demand of that shard.

When GOPS assignments change due to traffic changes, machine suspensions and other causes, Ghost will update the Edge Serial or Long Tail assignment for objects it serves from cache.  This is necessary to keep cache balances aligned.  Moving objects from Edge Serial to Long Tail will keep the long tail on correct machines.  Moving from Long Tail to Edge Serial keeps temporary Long Tail spread from filling the cache with objects that are unlikely to be subsequently requested. This will only happen as objects are requested \- there is no “reassignment scan” planned.  Whether reassignment happens only on refresh requests or on all cache hit requests is yet to be decided.  The objects moved will by definition be more popular and thus at the head of any eviction LRU regardless of the cache assignment. The overhead of a GOPS lookup might be avoidable for non-refresh requests.  We will evaluate this question as part of the Tech Preview.

### 2.4.3. GOPS and Long Tail Serials {#2.4.3.-gops-and-long-tail-serials}

GOPS, the Ghost Object Placement System, will be a new way of assigning content to servers. This system will be used both in this Region Distributed Cache system and in the future Split Ghost Dedicated Server Roles implementation.  GOPS will take a new hash of the inputs to the Ghost Cache Key MD5 hash and use a portion of the hash result to index a new DNSP message. This index will be called the GOPS Content Shard Index. The new message will have serial to IP mappings with much greater than the existing 4096 bucket serial number lookup.  Research into load balancing traffic across serials shows that the more serials used the more equal the distribution of load across the shards becomes. Using 256K shards provides a much better distribution than 32K shards, which is substantially better than the current DNSP range of 2K or 4K shards.

The long term goal is to report load located by GOPS on a new sub-service of the region’s Ghost service. Load for Long Tail requests will be reported on the new service so that DNSP can do the proper load balancing.  This will require work from Ghostmon, DNSP and Ghost to be fully supported so the Tech Preview will use a different method described in the Tech Preview section below.

### 2.4.4. Content Shard Index Selection {#2.4.4.-content-shard-index-selection}

The proposal for the new Ghost GOPS location hash is documented in the [GOPS Shard Index Construction And Ghost's Cache Key](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1D3Sjr_EMfDnDoDzP9t9QN6sZ4ohGRJNedikLwmoj3N8/edit)design document.  This is a work in progress but we expect the final design to closely resemble the document. The current Ghost cache key is an MD5 of several data fields that is then modified to allow for certain cache key variations to share the same base cache key. The summary of the document is that the same inputs to the current Ghost cache key hash will be combined with some of the post-hash modifications as inputs to a SHA256 hash. We want some sets of variations to hash to the same GOPS result, where other variations should hash to different server results.  For example, Ghost can cache variations on cache keys based on HTTP request methods like GET and HEAD.  These variations should be located on the shard index to maximize performance. Other variations like POC fragment should not share the same shard index in order to spread content across the Long Tail.  Using a better mapping solves some long standing problems in Ghost around cache key sharing where ICP can’t always find objects that have variations with distinct cache keys, when requests could share those objects.    
By default the POC fragment number will be included in the Shard Index Hash.  This will distribute POC fragments across the shards. This is similar to distributing POC fragments to different servers via HSAF, or adding the fragment ID into the hash used to select MCH or CW serials. There are three reasons to do this. First, distributing fragments prevents a popular object from overloading a single long tail server with requests from many Edge Serial servers. Second, in the case of a server suspension or failure only a portion of the object will be lost. Finally, it provides a better method of evenly spreading disk space since a single large POC object will not dominate a shard. There will be a metadata control to disable this behavior because there is a similar control for HSAF that was added in 2019\. Having the content all on the same server has the potential benefit of better performance since all bytes come from the same server.

The design will designate a specific set of input bits in the GOPS hash result as the shard index base value.  That base value will then be masked by the GOPS range to get the shard index. We can also designate several distinct subsets of the hash to provide alternative indexes or for use in bloom filter style lookups if that ever becomes desirable. Using a new hash also creates an eventual path to move away from the existing MD5 cache key hash if that becomes necessary.

### 2.4.5. Mapping URLs to the Long Tail {#2.4.5.-mapping-urls-to-the-long-tail}

The [How Shards Spread](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1o1YQvyJn3HnhBRzN6S2N1VXavlO82zs2gOXPTrgPzUY/edit#)design describes how we expect Content Shards to be mapped to IP Addresses.  Having calculated the shard index Ghost will look that index up in the GOPS DNSP message and get back a set of IP addresses.  The Shards document describes five methods for selecting a Long Tail IP from among the returned addresses based on different methods for distributing duplicate objects in the Long Tail.  In this document we will not discuss the options for selecting among multiple IP options specifically. Each method has a different expected replication rate for objects.  Deciding which selection method to use for production will be the subject of testing and analysis during the Tech Preview and later deployment phases. There will be configuration options to select different replication rates for customer footprint based on policies. Initially the selection policy will come from RDC system configuration controlled by Akamai engineering. Long term we may expose these policies for use in product templates of specific products that have content that could benefit from a particular replication rate enforced by a selection policy.  We want to avoid embedding selection policies in Customer ARL files because that quickly becomes an unscalable mess. The replication policies will initially be restricted to use in metadata files and channels controlled by Akamai engineering and operations rather than by customers.

### 2.4.6. Request Processing {#2.4.6.-request-processing}

On incoming requests Ghost will calculate the Shard Index for the object and look up that shard in the GOPS DNSP message to get an IP set.  On a cache miss or refresh rather than using ICP or HSAF/HSAR to determine an in region IP Ghost will use the configured GOPS selection method to find a Long Tail IP.  The Edge Serial Server will make a request to the Long Tail Server for the object.  The Long Tail Server will serve the object from cache or fetch the object from out of region. The response from the Long Tail IP will have the Edge Serial cache entrance criteria applied and if the object is selected to be persisted in cache it will be written to disk and marked as an Edge Serial object. On the Long Tail IP the object will be marked as a Long Tail Object.  

### 2.4.7. Multiple GOPS IPs {#2.4.7.-multiple-gops-ips}

We expect DNSP to return at least two IPs for each GOPS shard. The GOPS selection methods will choose one of those IPs as the Long Tail IP for any request. In the initial RDC implementation if the Edge Serial request to that Long Tail IP is a cache miss, the Long Tail IP will always go out of region for content.  This will result in a number of extra out of region requests if the object is sufficiently popular and the GOPS selection method distributes requests to both returned IPs.  We have decided that using ICP for this in the common case of low popularity objects is not worth the extra latency and CPU ICP would require. Other alternatives like the Edge Serial sending additional Long Tail IPs forward, or the Long Tail trying other IPs in the shard would likely end up creating extra copies of objects on Long Tail servers. If the out of region parent is a local Metrocache the latency added by the extra forward request will be minimal.  GRCP will provide additional optimizations that will solve this problem more efficiently.

### 2.4.8. Edge Serial Cache and Entrance Criteria {#2.4.8.-edge-serial-cache-and-entrance-criteria}

The Edge Serial Cache will create a degree of duplication of popular objects to improve TTFB for content whose edge serial might have spread.  As Edge Serials spread and unspread popular objects will move with the spread.  However, these popular objects will evict only other Edge Serial popular objects, not Long Tail objects.

If the Edge Serial cache writes every object from every Edge Serial request to disk it is likely that the amount of disk IO would be more than is sustainable.  It would prematurely age solid state storage and it would decrease offload.  Excessive write loads also cause disk IO head of line blocking because writes take longer than reads.  To prevent this we will have a system that decides which objects are persisted to disk, while other objects will be discarded shortly after they are served. We will reserve a portion of memory for holding unpopular objects that do not meet the entrance criteria for a short time, in case a subsequent request arrives that also wants the same object.

Some media clients use range requests within larger objects that mean that ranges within an object might be requested many times by a single client.  Although these ranges represent a single playback session they could quickly overwhelm popularity measurements and make all objects very popular when they are not.  Our entrance system will take this into consideration in the design to prevent this false positive signal, and any other false positives we find.

Recent research has shown that there is a set of outlier popular objects that can make a small subset of content shards very busy.  We need to make sure that these popular objects are preferred in the Edge Serial Cache to prevent their demand from overwhelming the Long Tail with demand.  Entrance criteria will need to have an understanding of Long Tail popularity and prefer caching objects with higher popularities over those with lower popularities. 

We will need to measure the effectiveness of the entrance criteria in caching the most useful objects to determine the best configuration of the system.  This will be one of the goals of the Tech Preview.

### 2.4.9. Prefetching and In-Region Amplifications {#2.4.9.-prefetching-and-in-region-amplifications}

With current implementations Ghost features like HTML parsing based prefetching, POC prefetching can generate significant in-region load that may have limited benefit if combined with features like P-ICP or if follow up requests for prefetched objects don’t arrive.  This system will have additional features to prevent these amplifications, while landing prefetched content at the correct machine in the Long Tail. This way if the follow up request goes to another machine in the region prefetching will still work without generating useless backend traffic. The details are described in detail the [Region Distributed Cache Amplification Avoidance](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ImFDLXPt8_1kUo5taipP5phEOKP_4wGPIM0Ros4uuGA/edit#)document. 

The brief description is that prefetch requests will be made to the Long Tail Servers with special indications that these are prefetch requests.  The Long Tail will populate its cache and return an HTTP 201 or 204 response code that indicates the object is in cache without returning data bytes.  Subsequent end user requests will trigger the prefetched bytes to move to the proper Edge Serial machine for delivery.  

### 2.4.10. Footprint Management Improvements {#2.4.10.-footprint-management-improvements}

The current footprint management system in Ghost does not scale well and needs improvements. This may become an issue with Large Regions that serve a diverse customer set.  With the Long Tail cache model the amount of cache available to any single footprint expands from the aggregate of that on all the machines in it’s Edge Serial to all of the disk space in the region.  Some customers with very long tails may end up consuming too much space and forcing out content for other customers.  It may become necessary to constrain disk footprint usage for customers to a limited amount of disk space.  This is not significantly different from today if we have customers using HSAF, HSAR, or on Metrocache parents.  If we need to make more specific quotas then the footprint system will need to scale better than it does today.  This work is needed in Ghost today so it is not specifically called out here as a requirement.  This implementation will probably make that work more urgent at some point.

## 2.5. Data collection and metrics {#2.5.-data-collection-and-metrics}

We need to be able to collect data on how the system performs.  These are some of the metrics we will need to be able to collect and compare to existing implementations:

1. Edge Serial cache statistics  
   1. How fast the Edge Serial cache is evicting \- ES Cache eviction age.  
   2. Edge Serial hit rates, disk read and write rates, net to disk ratios and similar metrics  
   3. Edge Serial cache entry rates for any solution that limits cache entry  
   4. Rates of Long Tail fetch failures that force the Edge Serial server to go out of region.  
   5. In-memory object eviction age and object re-use rates.  
2. Long Tail cache statistics  
   1. Hit rates and offload percentages  
   2. Eviction rates and overall eviction data.  
   3. Disk IO rates.  
3. End user performance  
   1. Overall throughput and TTFB for the region  
   2. In comparison to standard regions.  
4. Overall offload for the combined solution.  
   1. Standalone, and aggregate across the whole region   
   2. In comparison to regions using standard distribution approaches.  
   3. Performance of Edge Serial hits to Edge Serial misses that hit the Long Tail, or that miss completely.  
5. Serial loading for GOPS serials  
   1. Measure how even the disk footprint, bit load and flit load are distributed across the GOPS serials.  
   2. Identify the size and frequency of any hot or cold spots among the GOPS serials, and the difference between common minimum and maximum IO loads and cache footprints.  
   3. Experiment with the various GOPS selection strategies to decide if we can pick an effective default.  
6. CPU costs and savings  
   1. Cost of in-region fetches  
   2. CPU use reduction of no ICP vs cost of GOPS  
   3. Overall CPU flit usage vs standard deployment   
   4. Predict the constrained resource \- disk, CPU or network utilization.

## 2.6. Advantages and challenges of this solution {#2.6.-advantages-and-challenges-of-this-solution}

This solution has several advantages over the existing in region content distribution systems available today.  These include:

* All machines in the region participate equally in the Long Tail which gives that cache a much larger aggregate available disk space than traditional serial spread allows.  
* By using a large number of GOPS serials the Long Tail cache will keep objects in locations that vary little over time.  This means that the contents of Long Tail and the load it generates will be more stable than the Edge Serial load, which might vary based on end user demand.  
* Popular objects will quickly spread with Edge Serial spread, and then be evicted as demand drops on those serials.  Objects will be cached closer to Edge Serial demand, and will never be more than one predetermined location away from the end user.  With HSAF and P-ICP it is common to take two in-region hops for content.  
* It prevents existing traffic amplifications from prefetching and P-ICP.  
* It can allow us to spread Edge Serials greater than the existing default of two IPs.  This will increase Edge Serial cache duplication but will allow us to have more capacity available for spiky traffic.  We will reduce the effect of content locality on Edge Serial load spread which should allow for more load balancing flexibility.   
* We will have many tunings available to internally manage object duplication, Long Tail serial spread, Edge Serial cache size and admission policies and so on.  All of these will be internally tuned by Akamai without touching customer configurations.  
* We will be able to use this system for all sizes of regions from small AANP deployments to large data centers. This will allow us to decommission complex load distribution metadata from customer ARL files and product templates. Tuning and managing these systems becomes an entirely internal activity separated from customer ARL changes. Centralization will allow us to make tunings without changing customer configurations based on the needs of our deployments.  
* All of the code for GOPS, GRCP, Edge Serial Cache entry, amplification prevention and other features will be directly re-usable for Split Ghost.   
* The system will be designed to interact correctly with existing systems like Metrocache, Cloud Wrapper and others.

There are some notable challenges of the new system that we will need to carefully address. These include:

* With larger regions the odds of an overlap between Edge Serial IP and Long Tail IP is small. If the Long Tail lookup yields a single IP the odds that the IP return in the same as the Edge Serial machine is 1/(region size \-1). The larger the region the smaller the amount of content that overlaps.  Thus most Edge Serial cache miss traffic will need to go to a different server to access the Long Tail Cache.  With no optimizations these fetches will require the Long Tail IP  to execute a full stack request to serve content to the Edge Serial server.  CPU used to deliver bytes to Long Tail requests is not available for End Edge Serial delivery so we should be as efficient as possible in-region.  
* Tuning the system as it is initially deployed may be complicated.  It will definitely be a learning experience for all involved.  
* There may be some customer footprints that would be better served not distributing their cache across the region.  Without an opt-out policy from ARL that could be contained in a product template it will be too complicated to manage RDC for individual customers.   
* We may need footprint management to prevent large footprint customers from crowding out content that needs space for content with different re-use rates.  We will eventually need a footprint management system that is more flexible and scales better than the current system.  
* The fully optimized GRCP fetch will not be available until well after this system will be in production, so we will have to live with higher CPU costs for longer than we may have hoped.  
* Enabling this solution will have to automatically override customer configurations for in-region content distribution. Moving customers with complex solutions to this solution may be complicated.  
* Popularity based cache entrance systems can get quite complicated and may make bad predictions as to what to retain.  There are end user request patterns that tend to break simple popularity schemes that needs to be accounted for in the designs.  
* The solution adds complexity for deployments like Metrocache and Cloud Wrapper where it overlaps some of those distribution systems.  We must make sure that the Region Distributed Cache solution works well with the existing content aggregation solutions.  
* There may be some complexity when the system interacts with some customer behaviors like origin changes to cacheability (response based cacheability/no-store), 0s and similarly low TTLs, and very large footprint traffic.  
* Migrating from this solution to a true Dedicated Server Roles solution may be complex.  This migration has not been designed yet but will come into play in the future.

Other issues include more complexity for the code, more complex system level configurations like Footprint Descriptors, and potential interactions with all of the other Akamai caching related features. 

# 3\. Interaction With Existing Features {#3.-interaction-with-existing-features}

This system has to interact well with all of our existing cache distribution features. There are several key ones that need to be considered.

## 3.1. Rollout and production considerations {#3.1.-rollout-and-production-considerations}

At a minimum for rollout and likely for long term feature management there must be a way to allow flows to opt in or out of this system. The Tech Preview will give us experience with how the system works with a set of large customers, and we can probably use it to experiment with a limited set of other customer types as well.  When we start normal production deployments we will have to develop a strategy for converting existing regions from using the existing ICP, HSAR, HSAR and P-ICP feature sets to using the new RDC features.  Simply flipping a region-wide switch will cause a midgress and disk eviction burst as content shifts from being located based on Edge Serial spread to being located based on   
 We will undoubtedly need to do some sort of managed rollout that starts with a small portion of the content using the new system, eventually scaling up to the bulk of content in the region.  

## 3.2. Excluding content from the system {#3.2.-excluding-content-from-the-system}

### Ultra Low TTLs

There are a few content types that are not well suited for using the Region Distributed Cache model and should be excluded from having the features applied. An example of this is content with very low TTLs, zero second to a low number of seconds or subsecond TTLs.  These types of content don’t benefit from cache aggregation since they have a very high refresh rate. With low TTLs  there is a high risk of fetching or refetching stale objects from the Long Tail.  The RDC configuration should exclude any object whose TTL is below a threshold assigned in a system configuration. Zero second TTL should always be excluded since it is intended to force an origin revalidation.

### Ghost Generated Content

Many Ghost features create content that is subsequently cached.  Much of this content is not easily reused or has a relatively short lifetime, like ESI output, manipulated manifests, constructed responses and similar features.  With only a few exceptions these manipulations happen on the edge server that clients contact, not on parents.  It is rare that these objects are large \- all the processing features tend to have output size limits.  In an RDC deployment this means it would be created in the context of an Edge Serial request, not a Long Tail request.  Content of this type will not be pushed to the Long Tail.  It will stay on the Edge Serial server that created the object and will be subject to Edge Serial eviction rules.  If it is requested on multiple Edge Serial servers they will each produce the content. This is not substantially different from the current situation with generated content.  Features do not go forward for the generated content, but they will use the content that is in cache due to a prior request triggering the generation code.

### Sureroute Race Objects

Sureroute race requests will not go through the Long Tail.  This is to be expected if they are not cacheable, since uncacheable requests bypass the Long Tail cache.  Objects fetched by Sureroute races will, if they are cacheable, be cached as Edge Serial objects.  Typically they are not allowed in cache. 

## 3.3. Origin Determined cacheability and TTLs {#3.3.-origin-determined-cacheability-and-ttls}

Some customers use response headers to decide cacheability or set TTLs.  There are two types of doing this, both of which are problematic for any distributed cache mode.  The first type is that they could start content out as no-store, then make it cacheable based on a response header. The other type is to start content as cacheable and make it no-store or bypass-cache via a response header. It is difficult for Ghost to do something optimal in both of these cases without some sort of hint from metadata about best behaviors.  Even with hints there will be corner cases that are not ideal.

The current Ghost behavior is to look up all cache keys in its local cache prior to going forward regardless of metadata configured cacheability in case there are origin cacheability directives.  If the object is in cache from a previous request and it has the proper response headers to make it cacheable it can be served to end users from cache. If it does not have the proper headers it will be deleted from cache.  If metadata enables the bypass cache setting, this lookup is not done. 

### 3.3.1. Cacheability enabled by responses {#3.3.1.-cacheability-enabled-by-responses}

When cacheability is enabled by origin response headers, Ghost will cache the object locally.  In this case when RDC is enabled, the non-cacheable state will cause the Edge Serial Server to go out of region for the content, skipping the Long Tail server. Once the response headers are checked the object will be cached as an Edge Serial object, subject to Edge Serial entrance criteria.  If it is persisted, it will continue to be served from that server.  
This is less than ideal since the Object could possibly be a candidate for the Long Tail.  Since it is not cacheable any Edge Serial Server will go out of region and potentially to the origin.  This could be more servers than is desirable.  The Edge Serial Cache will provide some degree of offload but the rate will entirely depend on the object’s popularity and the Edge Serial Cache eviction age. 

With RDC a popular object that has this behavior enabled will end up in the Edge Serial cache.  Any cache aggregation across the region will be lost. This is not that different from the current behavior since Ghost will not make ICP requests for uncacheable objects.  
It would be possible to add a hint metadata tag that enabled a request to the Long Tail for these objects. This would be something that would involve per-config manual management of the hint and thus is not very scalable, unless we were to add Portal support for the hint. It is not clear that this amount of effort would be sufficiently beneficial.  For this design we will not propose such a hint tag.  We can evaluate the utility of the feature as part of the rollout of RDC.

### 3.3.2. Cacheability disabled by responses {#3.3.2.-cacheability-disabled-by-responses}

In this use case a customer makes their content cacheable, but disables the cacheability by use of response headers or late stage no-store/bypass cache metadata.  This style of cacheability control forces the RDC model to always contact an extra machine.  The flow is that the Edge Serial machine goes forward to a Long Tail machine, which then goes forward out of region, eventually hitting the origin.  All of these hops consider the object cacheable due to metadata settings.  The response converts the object to uncacheable and returns it upstream.  We will incur the extra flit cost and latency of the hop to the Long Tail machine and end up without an object in cache on any machine.  If individual objects are sufficiently popular the extra flits will also have an effect on the spread of the shard these objects are assigned.  Reducing these extra costs will be difficult without some sort of policy indicator from a product or customer configuration.  We will need to monitor the costs as part of the Tech Preview.

## 3.4. Footprints and eviction tunings {#3.4.-footprints-and-eviction-tunings}

This feature will use an attribute in the footprint identifier (FPID) system to determine which objects are Long Tail and Edge Serial objects.  This attribute will be defined in the Footprint Identification system through a change to the fpdata.xml file.  In summary, support for a new attribute called \<config-type\> will be added to fpdata.xml.  A single fp-config will be added that defines the attributes of the Edge Serial Cache.  Initially this will just be the space percentage and object count percentage allocated to the Edge Serial cache.  There will be a single set of eviction tunings for the Edge Serial cache specified in the Footprint Identification system. Per-footprint configurations specified in maprule, arild and other matches in the FPID system will not be applied to Edge Serial Cache objects.  However, those tunings that are based on product templates will still apply to the content. 

## 3.5. HOC {#3.5.-hoc}

The HOC will function as normal for Long Tail fetches.  We will also reserve a portion of memory normally used by the HOC for Edge Serial objects that we are holding in memory rather than writing to disk. 

## 3.6. Existing distribution and prefetching features {#3.6.-existing-distribution-and-prefetching-features}

The existing cache spread and discovery management features intersect with the Edge Serial Cache and Long Tail Cache in interesting ways. In some cases they will need to be applied as configured but in many cases they will be overridden or altered.

### 3.6.1. ICP and P-ICP  {#3.6.1.-icp-and-p-icp}

For Edge Serial Cache misses ICP will not be used to discover content.  Since there is one location in the Long Tail  for any object an Edge Serial miss should contact that location directly rather than attempting any discovery.  Using ICP on Long Tail requests will be disabled as noted above. Given the nature of granular GOPS assignments it is unlikely that ICP could discover more content than a GOPS assignment will discover.   
Probabilistic ICP will be disabled and becomes moot if ICP also is disabled.  The concept of probabilistic caching will be used as an Edge Serial Cache admission policy and as part of some GOPS selection methods.

### 3.6.2. HSAF and HSAR {#3.6.2.-hsaf-and-hsar}

HSAF will be disabled in both Edge Serial and Long tail caches.  This feature intends to replace all of the functionality of HSAF. 

HSAR is a more complicated problem  This feature is implemented in customer ARL using a combination of generic metadata tags.  Unlike HSAF there is no single tag or code path that can be disabled and cleanly disable all HSAR implementations.  For customers using HSAR there will need to be manual interactions to disable the feature.  None of the Tech Preview customers use HSAR and the feature is not popular with customers.  After the Tech Preview we will develop a strategy for working with HSAR implementations to assure that they work properly with Large Regions and RDC.

### 3.6.3. POC, Prefetching and amplifications {#3.6.3.-poc,-prefetching-and-amplifications}

We need some special adaptations to Partial Object Caching and prefetching in order to prevent in-region amplifications. The details of these amplification related questions are in the [Region Distributed Cache Amplification Avoidance](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1ImFDLXPt8_1kUo5taipP5phEOKP_4wGPIM0Ros4uuGA/edit) document. 

The summary of the proposals is that for normal Ghost usage the point of prefetching of either POC fragments, objects embedded in HTML pages or manifest based segment prefetching is to have the content positioned on the machine for subsequent requests.  For the RDC model, rather than making sure the content is in the machine processing the Edge Serial request, we will make sure that the object is in the Long Tail cache so that it is available for subsequent Edge Serial requests.  This will be done by making requests marked as Edge Serial prefetch to the appropriate Long Tail server for the prefetched objects. If the Long Tail has a cache miss the requests will trigger a forward fetch to populate the Long Tail.  The Long Tail will not return the full object to the Edge Serial, it will instead return an HTTP response 204 response that indicates that the object is in the Long Tail cache.  This will prevent the transit of extra potentially bytes while making sure that the Long Tail is fresh for any subsequent Edge Serial requests.

### 3.6.4. Range Requests {#3.6.4.-range-requests}

Range requests present similar amplification problems that are discussed in the Amplification Avoidance document.  There are two cases, POC and non-POC objects that will be handled differently.

For POC objects, normal Ghost fetches each full POC chunk needed to satisfy the range request. In the new implementation the Edge Serial machine will not fetch the full chunk at the beginning of the range. Instead it will make a request to the Long Tail for the portion of the range that spans less than a POC chunk.  For the rest of the content the Edge Serial will make normal POC fetches for the portion of the data that covers the remainder of the client range.  This will allow us to make popularity decisions for each POC chunk while potentially improving TTFB for the beginning of the requested range. 

Currently Ghost promotes client ranges for non-POC objects to fetch the full object regardless of the location of the range in the object. This behavior is based on configurations in baseline metadata that could be overridden by customer ARL or product templates but in practice are never changed. In the new implementation the range will be tunneled to the Long Tail, which might promote the range on the forward request out of region.

### 3.6.5. Metrocache {#3.6.5.-metrocache}

The order of fetches between machines will be RDC Long Tail then MCH if both are configured.  That way Ghost will fetch from the Long Tail and then the Long Tail will fetch from a Metrocache parent. In practice it is not clear whether Large regions will simply replace Metrocache rather than acting as either MCH Edge or Parent.  The current consensus is that Large Regions will co-exist with MCH and serve as both the edge and parent with overlapping cache. Once we have sufficient deployment experience this may be re-evaluated based on the observed benefits of the combined solutions.  This design does not require or suggest a specific outcome at this time.  If an RDC Large Region is an MCH parent region it is not clear whether the serial of objects used by Metrocache will be of much benefit.  We may want to adjust the MCH hash to use the RDC hash with the smaller serial count, so that better server locality is achieved. We can also try sending MCH parent requests directly to the correct RDC Long Tail server to save extra hops. This would involve publishing the RDC region’s GOPS message to more regions which may have scaling challenges. 

# 4\. Work Required for this project {#4.-work-required-for-this-project}

## 4.1. Work needed for the full system {#4.1.-work-needed-for-the-full-system}

This system needs work in many areas of Ghost and related systems.  This is a brief summary of the work needed

### 4.1.1. GOPS Shard Index implementation {#4.1.1.-gops-shard-index-implementation}

We need to implement that system that created the new GOPS hash and the GOPS Shard Index.  These are described in the [GOPS Shard Index Construction And Ghost's Cache Key](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1D3Sjr_EMfDnDoDzP9t9QN6sZ4ohGRJNedikLwmoj3N8/edit?)document. That design is a first draft so there are likely to be changes as detailed design work progresses.

### 4.1.2. GOPS Support in DNSP {#4.1.2.-gops-support-in-dnsp}

We need support for the GOPS messages and GOPS load reporting.  This includes:

1. Subservice reporting support in Ghostmon and ghostmon\_lib so that Ghost can report main service load and subservice load from the same Ghost process.  
2. Finalize details of the new DNSP message and get it working.  This includes message sizes (index ranges) and related details  
3. DNSP support for subservice load balancing.  
4. Ghost lookups in the new message.  
5. Ghost reporting Long Tail load on the new service,

### 4.1.3. Quota support {#4.1.3.-quota-support}

The system for marking content as Edge Serial or Long Tail, defining the Edge Serial quota in the proper region matches, and managing Edge Serial eviction are all needed.  There are existing design documents for this system with relatively straightforward implementation requirements.

### 4.1.4. Configuration Channel and files {#4.1.4.-configuration-channel-and-files}

We need to create the RDC configuration channel and publish the initial configuration.  This is described in the [Region Distributed Cache Metadata and Metrocache Interactions](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1IcOQdEPfoVOfAlnd5zMWHREAMZtZQfhxLzgKk4qArCw/edit#)document.  It is a relatively straightforward change to Ghost to add this channel.

### 4.1.5. Special Long Tail request types {#4.1.5.-special-long-tail-request-types}

We need to create the special Long Tail request type and support it in Ghost.  This also includes the changes to prefetching and other features to support the amplification prevention design, and for popularity indicators in Long Tail responses. 

### 4.1.6. Amplification Prevention {#4.1.6.-amplification-prevention}

Build in the checks to prevent amplification for prefetching, POC ranges and non-POC ranges.

### 4.1.7. Entrance Criteria and memory-only objects. {#4.1.7.-entrance-criteria-and-memory-only-objects.}

Changes to support Edge Serial cache entrance criteria and special in-memory handling of objects that are not persisted to disk. 

### 4.1.8. Reporting and Query support {#4.1.8.-reporting-and-query-support}
