We will need to define all of the data in any reporting system that will allow us to measure the effectiveness of the system.

### 4.1.9. GRCP {#4.1.9.-grcp}

Design docs exist for the GRCP protocol but significant work is needed to get it from proposal to implementation.  This doc proposes that GRCP be deferred to a later development phase.  When GRCP is available the object fetch flows and will change significantly, from a Long Tail controlled fetch to an Edge Serial controlled fetch.  There are significant details that need design work.  That work is not discussed in this proposal.

## 4.2. Tech Preview  {#4.2.-tech-preview}

The Tech Preview is intended to be deployed by the end of 2021\.  This will be a region that can serve content for four customers that need to have been identified as candidates for removing their customer-facing sharding requirements.  They are Hulu, Amazon, Bytedance and Disney.  Each customer has challenges that have driven a portion of the requirements for this system.  
Because of the short timelines we will not have a chance to implement many of the long term features for the system.  These are the shortcuts that we will make for the Tech Preview:

### 4.2.1. Reduced Serial Range {#4.2.1.-reduced-serial-range}

We will not have the new GOPS messages available in DNSP or the proper subservice reporting needed to load balance the GOPS system.  Instead we will use part of the existing serial range from 2049 \- 4095\.  The downside of this is that long tail serial usage won’t be as granular and we are likely to have greater issues with load imbalances on Long Tail serials. We should be able to get a good idea of the distribution effects and get a good idea of what benefits a larger serial range will yield.  GOPS shard hash computation and shard ID selection and the GOPS IP selection methods will be implemented.  Ghost will just look up IPs in the existing W service DNSP message using a smaller serial range.  

### 4.2.2. Full Stack Fetches {#4.2.2.-full-stack-fetches}

The LongTail requests will be normal G2G HTTP requests that apply full stack metadata and features.  These will use more CPU than is desirable but will populate the Long Tail cache on the correct servers. Replacing these fetches with the GRCP protocol is the longest pole of the whole development effort.  Until GRCP is ready we will need to carefully monitor the flit usage of Long Tail delivery and make whatever incremental changes we can to make them more efficient.  
In the Tech Preview the fetch path will always be Edge Serial \-\> Selected IP \-\> out of region parent or origin.  No attempt will be made to use additional IPs in the region on Long Tail requests. The Edge Serial will only use additional IPs if the first IP tried gets an error. In case of errors on all available Long Tail IPs the Edge Serial server will go out of region.  
We will need to monitor in-region connection usage to make sure that the machines are properly configured and can find enough available established connections to avoid excess connection latency.

### 4.2.3. Edge Serial Entrance Criteria {#4.2.3.-edge-serial-entrance-criteria}

Developing the Edge Serial Cache Entrance Criteria will be to be one of the longer projects.  We are likely to use a simple probability system in the interim.  This system will be susceptible to false positives and may not be able to get ideal offloads numbers. We will measure Edge Serial Cache effectiveness and try to tune the system as well as possible while we wait for improvements. 

### 4.2.4. Amplification Prevention {#4.2.4.-amplification-prevention}

The Tech Preview may not implement the amplification prevention strategies, depending on the availability of developer resources.

## 4.3. Future Work {#4.3.-future-work}

Looking forward to Split Ghost with Dedicated Server Roles there is some future work for this system.  These are a few items we will need to resolve:

* GRCP.  Implement GRCP and the fetching and consistency models it creates.  Using GRCP will eliminate the use of full stack requests for in-region fetches.  This will be a major CPU flit savings.  
* ESSL Support. As noted in section 2.2, ESSL deployment is out of scope for now, primarily because of persistent connection issues.  However, ESSL is likely to be in scope very soon after the Tech Preview so additional design work will be needed.  
* Transition planning for Dedicated Server Roles. If we implement Large Regions, what is the transition plan to move a deployment using RDC to a deployment using Dedicated Server Roles? This has not been thoroughly designed, but there is no expectation of incompatibility between the two solutions.  
* Transition Planning for AANP and small edge regions to using RDC to distribute in-region caching.  Ideally this system will replace all of the existing in-region content distribution implementations and we will need to transition existing regions at some point.
